_comment: Nested, location-aware rules. Two kinds: 'base' = the HumanityOS base set (distilled from the Humanity Accord, docs/accord/) which is where we want to GO; 'real' = plain-language summaries of real-world laws (where we ARE) with a source to verify. The point is a SMALL memorable base set plus condensed real laws, not millions of statutes. Data-driven (infinite-of-X): add jurisdictions + rules here, the app picks them up. Content focus: the operator's location (Silverdale, Kitsap County, WA, USA). See docs/design/laws.md.
disclaimer: Plain-language summaries for learning, not legal advice. Real laws change and vary by place; always verify with the cited source before relying on one. The HumanityOS base set is our own framework (where we want to be), not the law of any government.

rules.title: Where we want to be: few rules, clearly understood
rules.summary: Today there are millions of laws no person can remember, and they change constantly. The HumanityOS base set is the opposite: a small number of plain rights and limits, the same for every human, that you can actually hold in your head. Real laws (below) are where we are; this set is where we are going.
rules.source: Humanity Accord: purpose

rules.title: You control your own choices
rules.summary: You have agency and self-determination: control over your own actions, the right to refuse coercion or manipulation, and meaningful choice without hidden constraints.
rules.source: Humanity Accord: Rights and Responsibilities, Fundamental Right 1

rules.title: Your private life is yours
rules.summary: You have privacy of personal communication, confidentiality of your beliefs, votes, and associations, and protection from surveillance without your consent.
rules.source: Humanity Accord: Rights and Responsibilities, Fundamental Right 2

rules.title: You deserve the truth about systems you rely on
rules.summary: You have the right to accurate claims about what a system can do, clear disclosure of its limits and risks, and protection from deceptive assurances. Claiming security or privacy that does not exist is itself a harm.
rules.source: Humanity Accord: Rights and Responsibilities, Fundamental Right 3

rules.title: Your voice in decisions is protected
rules.summary: You can take part in collective decisions safely, with protection against vote tampering or suppression and assurance that your participation is counted accurately.
rules.source: Humanity Accord: Rights and Responsibilities, Fundamental Right 4

rules.title: One person, one voice
rules.summary: Everyone has equal standing: no amplification, suppression, or substitution of voices, and no privilege based on wealth, power, or access.
rules.source: Humanity Accord: Rights and Responsibilities, Fundamental Right 5

rules.title: You can always leave
rules.summary: You have the right to disengage, to revoke consent, and to withdraw without penalty. Participation must stay voluntary.
rules.source: Humanity Accord: Rights and Responsibilities, Fundamental Right 6

rules.title: Your rights come with responsibilities
rules.summary: Rights and responsibilities exist together. You are responsible for not violating others' rights, for not ignoring harm you can prevent, and for being truthful about what you do. Freedom without responsibility becomes harm to someone else.
rules.source: Humanity Accord: Rights and Responsibilities

rules.title: Some acts are never allowed
rules.summary: Certain acts are prohibited without exception, regardless of intent, belief, culture, authority, or claimed benefit, because they destroy the conditions for human dignity and safety. These include non-consensual sexual acts; they cannot be consented to or justified by any outcome.
rules.source: Humanity Accord: Absolute Prohibitions

rules.title: Government cannot punish most speech
rules.summary: The First Amendment stops the GOVERNMENT from punishing you for most speech, religion, press, assembly, and petition. It does not force a private person or company (including an online platform) to host your speech, and it has limits such as true threats and incitement.
rules.source: U.S. Constitution, First Amendment

rules.title: If accused, you have rights
rules.summary: You have the right to remain silent, the right to a lawyer, and the right to fair process before the government can take your life, liberty, or property. You do not have to answer questions without a lawyer.
rules.source: U.S. Constitution, Fifth and Sixth Amendments

rules.title: Police usually need a warrant or your consent to search
rules.summary: The government generally needs a warrant or probable cause to search you, your home, or your phone. You can say you do not consent to a search. There are exceptions (for example plain view or emergencies), so this is a general rule, not a guarantee.
rules.source: U.S. Constitution, Fourth Amendment

rules.title: You cannot be discriminated against on protected grounds
rules.summary: In jobs, housing, and public places it is illegal to discriminate against you based on race, color, religion, sex, national origin, disability, or age. The exact rules and who they cover vary by law.
rules.source: Civil Rights Act of 1964, Americans with Disabilities Act, and related federal laws

rules.title: You can vote at 18, and it cannot be denied by who you are
rules.summary: U.S. citizens 18 and older have the right to vote, and it cannot be denied based on race, sex, or age (18+). Most places require you to register first, and rules for registration and ID vary by state.
rules.source: U.S. Constitution, 15th / 19th / 26th Amendments; Voting Rights Act

rules.title: Washington has no personal income tax
rules.summary: Washington does not tax personal income. The state funds itself mostly through sales tax and other taxes instead, so your paycheck has no state income tax withheld (federal taxes still apply).
rules.source: Washington State Department of Revenue

rules.title: Washington has one of the highest minimum wages
rules.summary: Washington sets a state minimum wage that rises with inflation every year (well above the federal minimum). Some cities set it even higher. Check the current figure with Labor and Industries.
rules.source: RCW 49.46; Washington State Department of Labor and Industries

rules.title: You earn paid sick leave, and there is paid family leave
rules.summary: Most Washington workers earn paid sick leave (at least 1 hour per 40 hours worked), and the state runs a Paid Family and Medical Leave program that can pay you while you care for a new child or a serious health condition.
rules.source: RCW 49.46.210 (sick leave); Washington Paid Family and Medical Leave

rules.title: You usually need everyone's consent to record a private conversation
rules.summary: Washington is an all-party-consent state: it is generally illegal to record a private conversation or phone call unless everyone in it consents. This is stricter than many other states. Public settings with no expectation of privacy differ.
rules.source: Revised Code of Washington (RCW) 9.73.030

rules.title: Recreational cannabis is legal for adults 21+
rules.summary: Adults 21 and older may buy and possess limited amounts of cannabis from licensed stores. Public use, driving impaired, and giving it to minors are still illegal, and it remains illegal under FEDERAL law.
rules.source: RCW 69.50 (Initiative 502)

rules.title: Renters have a right to a livable home
rules.summary: A landlord must keep a rental reasonably safe and livable (working heat, plumbing, locks, structural soundness). Tenants have defined rights around deposits, repairs, and the notice required before eviction.
rules.source: Washington Residential Landlord-Tenant Act, RCW 59.18

rules.title: No holding your phone while driving; buckle up
rules.summary: It is illegal to hold or use a phone in your hand while driving (hands-free only, even at a stoplight). Seat belts are required for the driver and passengers. Tickets are common for both.
rules.source: RCW 46.61.672 (distracted driving); RCW 46.61.688 (seat belts)

rules.title: You usually need a permit to build or remodel
rules.summary: Most new structures, additions, decks, electrical, and plumbing need a county permit and inspection. Rural properties often have extra rules for septic, wells, and shoreline. Check before you build.
rules.source: Kitsap County Department of Community Development (verify current code)

rules.title: Outdoor burning is limited, and bans happen
rules.summary: Outdoor and yard-debris burning is restricted, and burn bans are declared in dry months or poor air quality. Check whether a ban is active before lighting anything outdoors.
rules.source: Kitsap County / Washington Department of Natural Resources burn-ban notices (verify current)

rules.title: Quiet hours and noise limits
rules.summary: Local noise rules limit loud sound (amplified music, construction, persistent barking), especially at night. Specific quiet hours and limits are set in the county code; check yours before assuming.
rules.source: Kitsap County Code, noise ordinance (verify current chapter)

rules.title: Water and septic are regulated locally
rules.summary: In and around Silverdale, water service, septic systems, and stormwater near Dyes Inlet are regulated to protect Puget Sound. Connecting, expanding, or maintaining these often needs approval. Check with the county health and public works offices.
rules.source: Kitsap Public Health District; Kitsap County Public Works (verify current)
